title: Child-exit signal mirror defeated by launcher's own signal handlers (exits 0 instead of 128+n)

findingDetails.evidence: childProcess.on('exit') does process.kill(process.pid, signal) to mirror the child's signal, but installSignalHandlers() (:911-929) registers handlers for SIGTERM/SIGINT/SIGHUP/SIGQUIT. The self-sent catchable signal re-enters that handler; childProcess.killed is false for an externally-killed child, so it takes the if-branch with a once('exit') that never re-fires, a no-op kill on the dead pid, and an unref()'d 5s timeout. Heartbeat timer is also unref()'d (:379), so the event loop drains and the launcher exits 0. SIGKILL (uncatchable) still mirrors correctly.
findingDetails.why: The documented intent (':894-897', 'mirroring the signal') is silently lost for catchable signals: a daemon killed by SIGTERM/SIGINT/SIGHUP/SIGQUIT makes the launcher report a clean exit (0) instead of the signaled status (143/130/129/131), so a supervising runtime mis-classifies abnormal daemon termination as a normal shutdown. spec-memory avoids this via a launcherShutdownInProgress guard (mk-spec-memory-launcher.cjs:1338) that code-index lacks.
findingDetails.recommendation: Before re-raising in the child-exit handler, remove the launcher's own handler for that signal first (process.removeAllListeners(signal) or set process.exit(128 + os.constants.signals[signal])) so the mirror is not intercepted; or compute and process.exit() the signal-derived code directly instead of relying on self-kill for catchable signals.
